author: @wirebrushproductions1001
text:
Adding a pipe will work, just not quite as well. You'll get frictional losses on the sides of the pipe.
Yes, if you turn it horizontally it will put out more power. That's because the way you've done it, air which would be diverted around one side now is trying to go under the vanes, but it can't because it is blocked by the table top. And if you try to use this for real, you now have to rotate the assembly to face the wind, and you lose the simplicity (no turning required) of the vertical mount.
And orienting it upside down will work, too. It will indeed make maintaining the generator assembly easier. And then you have to deal with the exhaust air, which is not a problem in the "normal" configuration as it gets shot up vertically.
TANSTAAFL - There is no such thing as a free lunch.

author: @guytech7310
text:
Instead of putting a curve into the fins:

1. Try putting an aerospike in the center to aid the airflow moving up. For a simple test put a vertical cylinder to see if that had any improvement, or make a cone out of paper.
2. Try angling the fins with a slight pitch to create an internal vortex (opposite to the fans rotation)
3. What happens if you block the fins on the far side (opposite to the fan)? 
4. Another trick is to put some posts on the corners some distance to the fan, as the posts can act like a wind lens increasing wind pressure. 
Using an anemometer for testing probably would provide a better means to gauge performance improvements than a PC fan.

author: @justtinkering6713
text:
Rob,
That's cool. Neat idea.
I really wish you would take a real close look at Peter Sharps "Cycloturbine' vawt.
It is like a Darrieus but the pitch of the blades is passively controlled. I have built several of these and I can testify that they 'pull like a truck'. Never fails to start turning and has enormous torque and tsr can go up to 3 or 4. His yt is tinkerbits ,. If you comment on his yt he will send you copies of his patents. Please check this out, it's amazing. If you want me to send you the docs provide a email address or some other way.

hi, very interesting, one thing about the profiles thought, when comparing to an airplane wing here we would be in an inversed stall situation. i wonder if the efficiency would increase if you printed the profiles with the curvature reversed so the aiflow follows the profile and gets guided upwards, in your case flow gets directed more to the center of the tube and only overpressure pushes the air upwards i think. 
the idea with a high tube/pipe and the fan on the bottom sounds great since higher up you catch more wind.

author: @giuseppebonatici7169
text:
better and cheaper than glue is welding, if you have a electronic soldering iron like the TS80 or similar, with a tip to spare (Its possible to clean afterwards anyway), you can configure the temps to 220-240°C (if you dont want PLA smoke in your face), and you can weld it to impresive results. Depending of the penetration, you can make a permanent weld o one that can be opened again with a knife, or a heated knife.
As with any welding technique, it takes times, and the weld strength will be limited to weld penetration (the layered structure is destroyed in the process, so with the right strategy, you can increase the overall strength of the 3d printed part.)  and somewhat with welding time (the polymer could start to go bad (the polymer chains could become shorter) if you take too long, but I have never tested it)

author: @fionahite170
text:
There are a lot of positive comments here. But, wouldn't you get more power if you removed all those slots that bend the wind?
This is a cute device but very inefficient. Why don't they put wind turbines close together? Because once the first turbine extracts the power from the wind, there is less left for the next turbine. There is a good reason this idea was in the dustbin of history, there are much better ideas out there. Sorry for the negativity but that's just the way it is.

author: @salimufari

author: @shiggins1324
text: Did you repeat the string experiment while the fan was sitting on top? With the fan on top does the air find a path of least resistance and start heading out the sides. author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Great video.  You COULD have called them streamers, though!  One of the things I like about this idea is thatt a small turbine could be incorporated into most houses and buildings without triggering anyone worried about appearance.  Even without the generator, use as downdraft ventilator could have a million uses.

author: @generalawareness101

author: @glencahalin4786
text: I'd expect 1/4 circle shaped  baffles to give a good redirection of air flow while producing less overall turbulence, and still being quick and easy to do in the 3D print program.

author: @ScandalistRick
text:
Put it on a swivel, give it a tail, then remove all of the vents from the 'dead' side.

I feel it would be more efficient, AND you would only have to print/assemble/glue half the amount of vents.

author: @livingladolcevita7318
text: How you managed to keep a straight face at 0.55 /0.56 is a wonder.🤣🤣 Seriously though would connecting a pipe to extend make it less efficient due to drag on the pipe. Time will tell I suppose.

author: @TheMarcusrobbins
text: I'm thinking you could do this from a piece of paper/thin plastic spiraled around along a length of string. Something you could pull open like those crazy expanding christmas decorations

author: @jacobopstad5483
text: I can see a potential problem with it upside down: any object that goes into it will immediately fall down into the pipe. With it right-side-up, there's very little danger of anything getting into it because of the upward slant. If you had a long tube of these, then you would get a multiplying effect and you could probably have the generator almost anywhere, even on the bottom, as it would create low pressure on the bottom.

author: @henningmogensen9144
text: It is the most interesting idea yet. One thing to point out: If uoy turn it upside down you will have to deal with rainwater ?

Did you ever read comics as a kid? Well I used to read the Beano and my favourite strip,was the Bash Street kids… “But Plug” was my favourite character… haha …

author: @jonbutcher9805
text: Ideas for future episodes. #1. Home made gas using home bio waste in a capped and vented hole in the ground. #2. Home made bio Fire logs made of paper, cardboard with optional sawdust or wood shavings mixed with water to pulp and put in easy to make moulds to dry of appropriate sizes.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: I like that it works in various orientations. Additionally, you make a very good point that when the fan is mounted at the bottom... it will be more easily serviceable. For long term protection, it will likely need some sort of wire fence to prevent birds from creating nests inside etc... It would take a really long time to 3D print this in a much larger size. But, because the parts can made in a modular fashion you could theoretically make it very large over time. I wouldn't use PLA long term outside, you might want to look into weatherproof and UV protected filaments if you plan to use the prints for outdoor experiment that you want to last.

author: @jamest.5001
text: I could see making something similar to this with straight sections, with a upwards curve, between two boards, cut a groove in the side boards, and bend a piece of plywood in the groove, glue or nail it together, then make more to make the final structure,

author: @shadowsilverlight1651

author: @tarasmostyka2027
text: I believe the longer the pipe will be, the more resistance to air it will cause. Eventually lowering generation to 0. Decreasing pipe diameter will make similar effect. It seems like threads on the back still show some air flow, so putting some membrane might increase the pressure inside.

author: @Absfor30
text: You wouldn't want one of these to operate upside down though as it would channel rainwater and snow into the centre wouldn't it? Or you could put a rain channel inside to divert the water away. It's a cool concept though, I wonder why on its side it's more efficient? Can you quantify how much more efficient it is? Perhaps it's simple laws of physics at play here with the weight of the air... but I reckon there is something else at play too...

author: @ibme8359
text: "Topsy -Turvy Turbine", I say, let's find a way to get the most efficiency from any way which way the wind blows. Perhaps some kind of controller 4 it!

author: @darrylbrown1319
text: I'm not sure if it was mentioned but a large benefit to driving the air down a pipe and mounting the generator close to the ground is the savings in copper/wire as well easier to install.

author: @Grynneman1
text: I'd be concerned about rain ingress if it was upside down.  Also, would it be as efficient as the original Darwin design?  I got the impression the Darwin design with its enclosed space would produce more air pressure within the cylinder by capturing and containing the breeze, whereas this design might allow air leakage from the negative air space behind the cylinder.

author: @chronosferatu345
text: I imagine that if the darwin turbine was upside down, though the effect would likely be small, gravity may help to move the air downward. Also with the turbine flipped, the air might be more easily used for other applications like passive air conditioning and not just producing electricity. I'm wondering if in addition to swooped fins if a swooped pyramidal tower, looking somewhat like the Eifel tower, inside the turbine would help move the air more efficiently.

author: @cathaloneill504
text: Nice to see we are like-minded. I have been working on a similar design to what you mentioned at the end, having the Darwin tower raised up, and having the wind travel down benefiting from the height difference to increase the velocity of the air. I tend to try experimenting soon, hopefully with some fellow university students.

replies.author: @xmysef4920
replies.text:
Are you sure about the ”height difference” part? Since air is everywhere, and the only reason it would fall down due to ”weight” is if it was in a medium that is less dense than itself, which is not how it is in our atmosphere. There is air everywhere, which means air can’t really just ”fall” in that sense.

The only exception is convection, but that doesn’t have anything to do with a height difference though.

author: @Dave_D.
text: I can't be the only one that did a double take at :53   LOL

author: @chrissscottt
text: Nice work. I wonder whether a hybrid design of a Darwin's wind turbine could be combined somehow with a 'solar updraught tower'. I might have a crack at making one.

author: @jimdaly5077
text: As I have said before this is not my field and so this will be ether so obvious or so stupid but,if the wind were to travel down a pipe, if put at the end of a pipe (a rocket stove) could this work. I realise it would need to be distant & protected from the heat🤔. 👍🐝🌞

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: Not to derail things here but i got a mental image of something like this inside the base of a rocket stoke to help increase the burn temps.

author: @paulmurgatroyd6372
text:
The only downside I can see for the downward facing baffles is that rain blown by the wind can get into the fan area much more easily than when it is pointing upwards.
I don't know a lot about small fan/generator assemblies, so I don't know if this would be a big problem or not.

author: @seabeepirate
text: The pipe idea made me think of trying to using this type of wind power to pull a slight vacuum using a Venturi nozzle. My mind went crazy with the task of optimizing the geometry to accelerate the air for stronger vacuum. My first thought on applications would be wind powered restroom vents. A linear actuator could operate a baffle to turn it on and off so it’s only using power during the switch from on to off and vice versa. I’m not well versed in fluid dynamics so there may not be enough velocity to create the suction.

author: @multiwtk
text: It seems like directing the airflow with curves would reduce resistance, the same reason curved turns are preferable in HVAC ductwork. With this in mind I think a curved cone inside the structure would also achieve the same effect.

replies.author: @CHUCK-C-BRUTAL-TRUTH

author: @SethCohn23
text: The round design is nice in that it'll capture wind from any direction... But the opposite side is open, meaning the wind energy is pushing air out those vents (less pressure).  Adding some way of "valving" those would increase the efficiency.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think you are wrong there mate - the back flaps because the air is wrapping around the pipe - but larger edge projections will tell us -w e don't have to guess lol

author: @SchwaAlien
text: There should be some kind of airflow simulation software available to use for cheap or free by now, I tried to use Blender a few years ago when I was optimizing a 2 fan to 1 pipe duct adaptor but it was a bit too much to figure out because it’s not set up to do that kind of function out of the box.  I went back to printing to test them but I could have done more versions digitally if I had figured out the airflow simulation, and it could really come in handy when testing and optimizing something truly new like this.

author: @CCoburn3
text: The pipe would be a cheap way to go. But you could just build a tall octagonal tower with louvers. That would mean more area exposed to the wind. But it might not be worth the cost. It looks like there is plenty of room for experimentation. Since your louvers are not curved, they don't need to be printed. Expanded PVC would be cheap and quick. Plus, you could build a much larger structure than a printer could fabricate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I did them uncured deliberately - curved would be better so as not to impede airflow I think - but not essential to get a good result

replies.author: @CCoburn3
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering But you got such good results from straight that curved might not be worthwhile -- at least not for the home builder. Straight is easy. A guy could make an entire tower in an afternoon with just a table saw, hammer, and nails.

author: @MerwinARTist
text: It seems to me that this piece would work better if the wind vanes were hinged and able to close .. so that the wind coming in one side would only be able to go up the middle.  What you have with the open vanes on the other side is a place where wind-pressure can escape.  If that energy could be forced to go up the middle .. seems like it would work better.  Easy enough to try it out by taping up the openings on one side.  It is a nice looking piece tho.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: apparently not mate - we have tried both - I think the fins need to extend out more - but it's only what I think - you could try it and see

replies.author: @MerwinARTist
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Maybe I will .. but for the sake of timely communication .. I think it would be better to have a wind wall .. say 4 - 6 ft. long and maybe 12 in. wide .. with a solid partition in the middle.  The two outside faces could have the openings .. facing the wind direction at the top of a roof.  The wind catcher could be laterally on the top.  I appreciate what you are doing .. scrambling the eggs!  lol :-)

replies.text: no - there was no point as there is no real point of reference in this to be able to compare them - for example how far is the fan from the wind source? They can be compared but that will be a little calculation and a few assumptions and that will be down the line for me - of course you could always do it if you felt you needed it - if you did - please share

author: @pauljenkinson1452
text: If you angle the fins so that a vortex is formed in the middle then it will cause a greater pressure difference due to the increase in velocity and suck more air in. It worked with your rocket stove. 🤔😉

replies.author: @AndyWhiteBrighton
replies.text: Second this 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting idea mat - thank you for sharing

replies.author: @pauljenkinson1452
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I do have a 3D printer based on your videos. I don't know if its possible what I am saying because with the rocket stove you have two effects, the heat causing convection internally causing a drop in pressure and if a horizontally rotating geometry if applied to in secondary air flow then this could increase the vortex air speed that will reduce the pressure further. However, with the Darwin structure you are already having the air flow as a primary driver. That being said, building a Darwin structure out of black heat absorbing material will not only get the benefit of wind but solar convection. Overall, what I would say is for wind power generation, the device wants to increase the aerodynamic drag effect thus increasing the pressure mediation thus increasing force in the system to then be converted into power.

author: @Xero1of1
text: I can tell you already that if you stick a pipe on the end of it, it'll work just fine. By inverting it, you made me recognize it as something else immediately. Thousands of years ago, people who lived in the desert found a way to capture wind to cool their homes. These were called 'Persian Wind Catchers' and were effective enough in cooling homes that people could live in desert areas for many centuries.

replies.author: @PaleHorseObscura
replies.text: Quite astute

replies.author: @southerneruk
replies.text: Caused an up draft of air.

replies.author: @Zagiman287
replies.text: I wonder just how much length of pipe you can get away with before experiencing any reduction in air flow through the pipe caused by drag and turbulence within the pipe. A similar phenomenon happens with a water hose and the pressure at the outlet of the hose. A 20 ft water hose will have pretty good pressure at its end in comparison with the source pressure, where as a 150' hose will have significantly reduced pressure at its outlet compared with the source pressure.

replies.author: @southerneruk
replies.text: @Zagiman287 Have you ever been at the bottom of a chimney stack, the higher it is greater the vacuum up draft

author: @AndyThompson1606
text: I had been thinking about the airial Venturi/ground converter idea for a while, originally to drive magnets beside a copper pipe to collect heat. That aside, you might see an increase in efficiency if you put a solid core in the 'beehive' to direct airflow to the outer portion of the turbine blades...

author: @401ksolar
text: You've created something that looks just like a vent cover that I have seen people put on a pipe all the way to their basement floor with a ventilation stack in the air as the wind blows over the top it creates a vacuum which sucks out the dense moisture Laden air from the basement floor thereby eliminating small pools of water that he thought were cracks in the basement floor, it's amazing how similar Your Design looks to that vent cap LOL

author: @actupper
text:
The pipe idea is interesting! Could almost imagine scaled up versions in car parks (parking lots) doing double duty as turbines and light poles. 

Another idea that the yarn test sparked for me was adding some kind of rotating half-barrel shield. It would rotate to the leeward side  of the tower, and help direct air which has flowed around the tower into the leeward inlets. Maybe not a huge performance gain, but interesting to mull over.

author: @TheBaconWizard
text: The fact that is works "upside-down" is handy to know. So, going back to your original design with the hinged flaps that were noisy... what if those flaps per replaced by a textile? Sail-cloth perhaps. Attached/hinged at the top as before, free to move ie open at the bottom, and a triangle of cloth at the sides to attach it and limit the movement. Any wind coming from the other side would close it for you. I reckon it would look like shark's gills opening and closing in the wind. This iteration might benefit from having the fan on the bottom.

replies.author: @kbjerke
replies.text: Just a quick observation here, but it seems to me that "wind" doesn't know the difference between "right-side up", nor "upside-down."  YMMV.

author: @seanchadwick6182
text: I wonder if an improvement would be to add some really lightweight flaps to the inside of each opening to prevent any air going straight out the other side. Even something just like paper that would hang down when no wind was blowing and rise up when the wind hits

replies.author: @goodboiadvsp3297
replies.text: He had a big model of that exactly in the last video he said there was a problem with noise. This is the next iteration. You can notice from the wool strings that there is no problem with the air escaping the other side, it is all directed upwards

replies.author: @seanchadwick6182
replies.text: @goodboiadvsp3297 not quite what I was getting at, on his previous model the flaps where made of ply and opened to allow the wind in. With this model that flap is essentially always open. It would be interesting to see if Rob where to attach the wool to the opposite side to where the air enters to see if any does escape out of the side. You need to.divert as much of that incoming air as possible.

replies.author: @goodboiadvsp3297
replies.text: @seanchadwick6182 Now I'm confused, at 2:06 you can see the opposite side to the fan the wool only wobbles a bit whereas the side with the fan completely gets pushed out of the way by the air. I think that proves pretty well almost none of the air is going directly through the model

replies.author: @seanchadwick6182
replies.text: @goodboiadvsp3297 carry that video on to around 2:13 and you can see the opposite side moving. It may not make much difference was just a thought. You don't want to be losing any precious wind power especy when working with lower speeds than a traditional turbine would work with.
